1.o: It is a mercy to have the word of God brought to us, and a duty to attend to it diligently, when we are in affliction. The voice of God came in the fulness of light and power, by the Holy Spirit. These visions seem to have been sent to possess the prophet's mind with great and high thoughts of God. To strike terror upon sinners. To speak comfort to those that feared God, and humbled themselves. In Ezek. 1:4-14, is the first part of the vision, which represents God as attended and served by a vast company of angels, who are all his messengers, his ministers, doing his commandments. This vision would impress the mind with solemn awe and fear of the Divine displeasure, yet raise expectations of blessings. The fire is surrounded with a glory. Though we cannot by searching find out God to perfection, yet we see the brightness round about it. The likeness of the living creatures came out of the midst of the fire; angels derive their being and power from God. They have the understanding of a man, and far more. A lion excels in strength and boldness. An ox excels in diligence and patience, and unwearied discharge of the work he has to do. An eagle excels in quickness and piercing sight, and in soaring high; and the angels, who excel man in all these respects, put on these appearances. The angels have wings; and whatever business God sends them upon, they lose no time. They stood straight, and firm, and steady. They had not only wings for motion, but hands for action. Many persons are quick, who are not active; they hurry about, but do nothing to purpose; they have wings, but no hands. But wherever the angels' wings carried them, they carried hands with them, to be doing what duty required. Whatever service they went about, they went every one straight forward. When we go straight, we go forward; when we serve God with one heart, we perform work. They turned not when they went. They made no mistakes; and their work needed not to be gone over again. They turned not from their business to trifle with any thing. They went whithersoever the Spirit of God would have them go. The prophet saw these living creatures by their own light, for their appearance was like burning coals of fire; they are seraphim, or "burners;" denoting the ardour of their love to God, and fervent zeal in his service. We may learn profitable lessons from subjects we cannot fully enter into or understand. But let us attend to the things which relate to our peace and duty, and leave secret things to the Lord, to whom alone they belong.
1.m:
Having the word of God come to us is a blessing, especially during hard times. We should listen carefully to it. 

God’s message came through the Holy Spirit with full clarity and power. These visions were meant to fill the prophet with thoughts of God’s greatness. 

The visions aimed to frighten sinners and comfort those who respected God and humbled themselves. 

In Ezekiel chapter 1, verses 4 to 14, the first part of the vision shows God surrounded by many angels who carry out His commands. 

This scene would inspire both fear and hope for blessings. The fire was encircled by a bright halo. Even though we can’t fully understand God, we can see the radiance around Him. 

The living creatures appeared from within the fire, showing that angels get their existence and power from God. They have human-like intelligence, but much greater. 

A lion symbolizes strength and courage. An ox stands for diligence and patience. An eagle represents sharp eyesight and speed. Angels surpass humans in all these qualities. 

Angels have wings to move quickly wherever God sends them. They stand upright and steady. 

They use their wings for movement and their hands for tasks. Some people rush around without getting anything done. They may have wings but lack hands. 

But whenever angels fly somewhere, they bring their hands along to work. They always go straight ahead, focused on their task. 

They don’t turn aside to waste time. Wherever the Spirit directs them, they follow without hesitation.

The prophet saw these living creatures glowing with their own light, like burning coals of fire. They are called seraphim, which means "burners." This shows how much they love God and how eager they are to serve him.

We can learn useful lessons even from things we don't fully understand. Focus on what helps you live right and find peace. Leave the mysteries to God, who knows everything.

1.o: Providence, represented by the wheels, produces changes. Sometimes one spoke of the wheel is uppermost, sometimes another; but the motion of the wheel on its own axletree is regular and steady. We need not despond in adversity; the wheels are turning round and will raise us in due time, while those who presume in prosperity know not how soon they may be cast down. The wheel is near the living creatures; the angels are employed as ministers of God's providence. The spirit of the living creatures was in the wheels; the same wisdom, power, and holiness of God, that guide and govern the angels, by them order all events in this lower world. The wheel had four faces, denoting that the providence of God exerts itself in all parts. Look every way upon the wheel of providence, it has a face toward you. Their appearance and work were as a wheel in the middle of a wheel. The disposals of Providence seem to us dark, perplexed, and unaccountable, yet are all wisely ordered for the best. The motion of these wheels was steady, regular, and constant. They went as the Spirit directed, therefore returned not. We should not have to undo that by repentance which we have done amiss, if we followed the guidance of the Spirit. The rings, or rims of the wheels were so vast, that when put in motion the prophet was afraid to look upon them. The consideration of the height and depth of God's counsel should awe us. They were full of eyes round about. The motions of Providence are all directed by infinite Wisdom. All events are determined by the eyes of the Lord, which are in every place beholding the evil and the good; for there is no such thing as chance or fortune. The firmament above was a crystal, glorious, but terribly so. That which we take to be a dark cloud, is to God clear as crystal, through which he looks upon all the inhabitants of the earth. When the angels had roused a careless world, they let down their wings, that God's voice might be plainly heard. The voice of Providence is to open men's ears to the voice of the word. Sounds on earth should awaken our attention to the voice from heaven; for how shall we escape, if we turn away from Him that speaks from thence.
1.m:
Providence, shown by the wheels, brings changes. Sometimes one part of the wheel is up, then another part rises. But the wheel keeps moving steadily. Don't worry during hard times; the wheels keep spinning and will lift you up eventually. Those who feel too confident in good times don’t realize how quickly they could fall. 

The wheels are close to the living beings, showing that angels help carry out God’s plans. The spirit of the living beings was in the wheels, meaning God’s wisdom, power, and holiness control both angels and earthly events. Each wheel had four sides, showing God’s care everywhere. Look at the wheel of life from any angle, it always faces you. 

God’s plans seem confusing to us, but they’re always wise. The wheels moved smoothly without stopping. They went where the Spirit guided them, so they didn’t return. If we follow the Spirit’s direction, we won’t have to fix mistakes later. 

The wheels were huge, making the prophet uneasy to watch them spin. Thinking about God’s great plans should humble us. The wheels had many eyes around them, showing that God directs everything with perfect wisdom. Everything happens because God sees it all clearly. There’s no luck or chance involved.

Above was a bright, clear sky, but also frightening. What seems dark to us is clear to God. When angels wake up sleepy people, they fold their wings back so God’s message can be heard. This shows that God wants us to listen carefully to His words. Listen to sounds around you and pay attention to God’s voice coming from heaven. How else can we avoid trouble if we ignore what He says?

1.o: The eternal Son, the second Person in the Trinity, who afterwards took the human nature, is here denoted. The first thing observed was a throne. It is a throne of glory, a throne of grace, a throne of triumph, a throne of government, a throne of judgment. It is good news to men, that the throne above the firmament is filled with One who appears, even there, in the likeness of a man. The throne is surrounded with a rainbow, the well-known emblem of the covenant, representing God's mercy and covenanted love to his people. The fire of God's wrath was breaking out against Jerusalem, but bounds should be set to it; he would look upon the bow, and remember the covenant. All the prophet saw was only to prepare him for what he was to hear. When he fell on his face, he heard the voice of One that spake. God delights to teach the humble. Let sinners, then, humble themselves before him. And let believers think upon his glory, that they may be gradually changed into his image by the Spirit of the Lord.
1.m:
The eternal Son, the second person in the Trinity, who later became human, is described here. The first thing noticed was a throne. This throne represents glory, grace, victory, rule, and judgment. 

It’s good news that the throne above the sky is occupied by someone who looks like a human. Around the throne is a rainbow, which symbolizes God’s promise and shows His mercy toward His people. Even though God’s anger was directed at Jerusalem, He promised to stop it. He would see the rainbow and remember His promises.

What the prophet saw was just preparation for what he was about to hear. When he bowed down, he heard a voice speaking. God enjoys teaching those who are humble. So, let sinners humbly approach Him. And let believers think about His greatness so they can become more like Him through the Holy Spirit.

2.o: Lest Ezekiel should be lifted up with the abundance of the revelations, he is put in mind that still he is a son of man, a weak, mortal creature. As Christ usually called himself the Son of man, it was also an honourable distinction. Ezekiel's posture showed reverence, but his standing up would be a posture of greater readiness and fitness for business. God will speak to us, when we stand ready to do what he commands us. As Ezekiel had not strength of his own, the Spirit entered into him. God is graciously pleased to work in us whatever he requires of us. The Holy Spirit sets us upon our feet, by inclining our wills to our duty. Thus, when the Lord calls upon the sinner to awake, and attend to the concerns of his soul, the Spirit of life and grace comes with the call. Ezekiel is sent with a message to the children of Israel. Many might treat his message with contempt, yet they should know by the event that a prophet had been sent to them. God will be glorified, and his word made honourable, whether it be a savour of life unto life, or of death unto death.
2.m:
To keep Ezekiel from becoming proud because of all the things he saw, God reminded him that he was just a regular person—a weak and mortal one. Like Jesus often calling himself the Son of Man, this was meant to honor Ezekiel. Ezekiel’s position showed respect, but standing up showed he was ready to do God’s work. 

Since Ezekiel couldn’t do anything on his own, the Spirit came into him. God helps us do what he asks us to do. The Holy Spirit makes us want to follow our duties. When God calls a sinner to wake up and pay attention to their soul, the Spirit gives them new life and grace.

Ezekiel was given a message for the people of Israel. Some might ignore his words, but later events would show that a prophet had spoken to them. Whether his message brought life or death, God would be honored either way.

2.o: Those who will do any thing to purpose in the service of God, must not fear men. Wicked men are as briers and thorns; but they are nigh unto cursing, and their end is to be burned. The prophet must be faithful to the souls of those to whom he was sent. All who speak from God to others, must obey his voice. The discoveries of sin, and the warnings of wrath, should be matter of lamentation. And those acquainted with the word of God, will clearly perceive it is filled with woe to impenitent sinners; and that all the precious promises of the gospel are for the repenting, believing servants of the Lord.
2.m:
People who want to serve God well shouldn’t be afraid of other people. Wicked people are like sharp thorns, close to being cursed. Their fate is to be destroyed by fire. 

The prophet needs to care about the people he’s sent to help. Anyone speaking God’s words to others must listen to what God tells them. When we learn about our sins and hear warnings about God’s anger, we should feel sad. 

Anyone familiar with God’s message knows it includes many warnings for those who don’t turn away from their sins. It also has good promises for those who truly believe and follow God.

3.o: Ezekiel was to receive the truths of God as the food for his soul, and to feed upon them by faith, and he would be strengthened. Gracious souls can receive those truths of God with delight, which speak terror to the wicked. He must speak all that, and that only, which God spake to him. How can we better speak God's mind than with his words? If disappointed as to his people, he must not be offended. The Ninevites were wrought upon by Jonah's preaching, when Israel was unhumbled and unreformed. We must leave this unto the Divine sovereignty, and say, Lord, thy judgments are a great deep. They will not regard the word of the prophet, for they will not regard the rod of God. Christ promises to strengthen him. He must continue earnest in preaching, whatever the success might be.
3.m: Ezekiel needed to believe and accept the truth from God as nourishment for his spirit. This would give him strength. People who love God enjoy learning about His teachings, even if those teachings warn others against sinning. Ezekiel had to share only what God told him. Using God’s exact words is the best way to express God’s thoughts. Even if people don’t listen, Ezekiel should stay patient. The people of Nineveh listened to Jonah, while the Israelites did not change their ways. We need to trust God’s decisions and say, “Lord, your judgments are beyond our understanding.” The people won't pay attention to the prophet’s message because they ignore God’s warnings too. Jesus promised to support Ezekiel. Ezekiel needed to keep preaching hard, no matter the results.

3.o: This mission made the holy angels rejoice. All this was to convince Ezekiel, that the God who sent him had power to bear him out in his work. He was overwhelmed with grief for the sins and miseries of his people, and overpowered by the glory of the vision he had seen. And however retirement, meditation, and communion with God may be sweet, the servant of the Lord must prepare to serve his generation. The Lord told the prophet he had appointed him a watchman to the house of Israel. If we warn the wicked, we are not chargeable with their ruin. Though such passages refer to the national covenant made with Israel, they are equally to be applied to the final state of all men under every dispensation. We are not only to encourage and comfort those who appear to be righteous, but they are to be warned, for many have grown high-minded and secure, have fallen, and even died in their sins. Surely then the hearers of the gospel should desire warnings, and even reproofs.
3.m:
This mission made the holy angels happy. It showed Ezekiel that the God who sent him had the power to help him do his job. Ezekiel felt sad because of the sins and troubles of his people, and he was overwhelmed by the glory of what he saw. Even though being alone, thinking, and talking to God feels good, servants of the Lord need to get ready to serve others.

The Lord told Ezekiel he chose him to be a watchman for the house of Israel. If we warn bad people, we aren’t responsible if they still end up ruined. These instructions apply not just to the nation of Israel back then, but to everyone today too. We should both support and cheer up those who seem good, but we should also warn them because some become proud and careless, fall from grace, and die in their sins. So, people who hear the gospel should want to receive warnings and even corrections.

3.o: Let us own ourselves for ever indebted to the mediation of Christ, for the blessed intercourse between God and man; and a true believer will say, I am never less alone than when thus alone. When the Lord opened Ezekiel's mouth, he was to deliver his message boldly, to place life and death, the blessing and the curse, before the people, and leave them to their choice.
3.m:
We should always be grateful for Jesus' work in bringing God and people together. A true believer will say, "I feel least alone when I'm by myself with God."

When God gave Ezekiel the ability to speak, he told him to share his message clearly. He was to show people the choices between life and death, blessings and curses, and let them decide.

4.o: The prophet was to represent the siege of Jerusalem by signs. He was to lie on his left side for a number of days, supposed to be equal to the years from the establishment of idolatry. All that the prophet sets before the children of his people, about the destruction of Jerusalem, is to show that sin is the provoking cause of the ruin of that once flourishing city.
4.m: The prophet was to show the siege of Jerusalem using signs. He was to lie on his left side for a certain number of days, which likely matches the years since idolatry began. Everything the prophet showed to the people about the destruction of Jerusalem was meant to prove that sin caused the downfall of that once-prosperous city.

4.o: The bread which was Ezekiel's support, was to be made of coarse grain and pulse mixed together, seldom used except in times of urgent scarcity, and of this he was only to take a small quantity. Thus was figured the extremity to which the Jews were to be reduced during the siege and captivity. Ezekiel does not plead, Lord, from my youth I have been brought up delicately, and never used to any thing like this; but that he had been brought up conscientiously, and never had eaten any thing forbidden by the law. It will be comfortable when we are brought to suffer hardships, if our hearts can witness that we have always been careful to keep even from the appearance of evil. See what woful work sin makes, and acknowledge the righteousness of God herein. Their plenty having been abused to luxury and excess, they were justly punished by famine. When men serve not God with cheerfulness in the abundance of all things, God will make them serve their enemies in the want of all things.
4.m:
The bread Ezekiel ate was made from rough grains and beans, usually only eaten during times of great need. He got very little of it. This showed how badly the Jewish people would suffer during the siege and while they were taken away.

Ezekiel did not complain, saying things like, "I've always lived well and never eaten anything like this." Instead, he said he had always tried to follow God’s laws carefully. 

It helps us feel better when we face hard times if we know we’ve always tried to do right. Sin causes terrible problems, and we should see how fair God is in punishing those who misuse their blessings. 

When people waste food and live wastefully because they have too much, God may punish them by making them go hungry. People who don’t serve God gladly when they have plenty might end up serving their enemies when they’re poor and lacking everything.

5.o: The prophet must shave off the hair of his head and beard, which signifies God's utter rejecting and abandoning that people. One part must be burned in the midst of the city, denoting the multitudes that should perish by famine and pestilence. Another part was to be cut in pieces, representing the many who were slain by the sword. Another part was to be scattered in the wind, denoting the carrying away of some into the land of the conqueror, and the flight of others into the neighbouring countries for shelter. A small quantity of the third portion was to be bound in his shirts, as that of which he is very careful. But few were reserved. To whatever refuge sinners flee, the fire and sword of God's wrath will consume them.
5.m: The prophet had to shave all his hair and beard, showing that God completely rejected and abandoned that people. Part of the hair was burned in the middle of the city, symbolizing those who would die from hunger and disease. Another part was cut up, representing those killed by swords. Some of the hair was scattered by the wind, showing how some people were taken away to other lands by their enemies, while others fled to nearby countries for safety. Only a little bit of the last part was kept safe, wrapped in his shirt, because only a few were saved. Wherever sinners try to run, God’s judgment will find them.

5.o: The sentence passed upon Jerusalem is very dreadful, the manner of expression makes it still more so. Who is able to stand in God's sight when he is angry? Those who live and die impenitent, will perish for ever unpitied; there is a day coming when the Lord will not spare. Let not persons or churches, who change the Lord's statutes, expect to escape the doom of Jerusalem. Let us endeavour to adorn the doctrine of God our Saviour in all things. Sooner or later God's word will prove itself true.
5.m:
The punishment for Jerusalem is very severe. The way it is said makes it even worse. Who can stand before God when he is angry?

Those who die without changing their ways will be lost forever. There will come a time when God will not show mercy.

People and churches should not think they can avoid the fate of Jerusalem if they ignore God’s rules.

We should try to honor God by living according to his teachings.

God’s word will always turn out to be true, sooner or later.

6.o: A remnant of Israel should be left; at length they should remember the Lord, their obligations to him, and rebellion against him. True penitents see sin to be that abominable thing which the Lord hates. Those who truly loathe sin, loathe themselves because of sin. They give glory to God by their repentance. Whatever brings men to remember Him, and their sins against him, should be regarded as a blessing.
6.m: Some people from Israel will be saved. Eventually, they will remember the Lord, what he has done for them, and how they have rebelled against him. People who really feel sorry for their sins see those sins as things the Lord hates. When someone truly regrets their sins, they also regret themselves because of those sins. Their sorrow shows honor to God. Anything that helps people remember the Lord and their wrongs against him is a blessing.

6.o: It is our duty to be affected, not only with our own sins and sufferings, but to look with compassion upon the miseries wicked people bring upon themselves. Sin is a desolating thing; therefore, stand in awe, and sin not. If we know the worth of souls, and the danger to which unbelievers are exposed, we shall deem every sinner who takes refuge in Jesus from the wrath to come, an abundant recompence for all contempt or opposition we may meet with.
6.m:
We should feel sorry not just for our own mistakes and problems, but also for the troubles that bad choices cause others. Sin causes destruction, so be careful and don't sin. 

If you realize how valuable souls are and how dangerous it is for those who do not believe, then you will see that helping a sinner find safety through Jesus is worth more than any trouble you might face.

7.o: The abruptness of this prophecy, and the many repetitions, show that the prophet was deeply affected by the prospect of these calamities. Such will the destruction of sinners be; for none can avoid it. Oh that the wickedness of the wicked might end before it bring them to an end! Trouble is to the impenitent only an evil, it hardens their hearts, and stirs up their corruptions; but there are those to whom it is sanctified by the grace of God, and made a means of much good. The day of real trouble is near, not a mere echo or rumour of troubles. Whatever are the fruits of God's judgments, our sin is the root of them. These judgments shall be universal. And God will be glorified in all. Now is the day of the Lord's patience and mercy, but the time of the sinner's trouble is at hand.
7.m:
This prophecy comes suddenly and repeats itself often because the prophet felt very strongly about the coming disasters. 

Such will be the punishment for sinners; nobody can escape it. It would be better if people stopped being bad before it’s too late!

For those who don’t turn from their sins, trouble just makes things worse. Their hearts get harder, and they become more sinful. But some use trouble as a chance to grow closer to God thanks to his grace. 

Real trouble is coming soon, not just rumors of it. Our sins cause God’s punishments. Everyone will face these judgments. 

God will be honored through everything that happens. Right now is the time of God’s patience and mercy, but the time of judgment for sinners is close.

7.o: Sooner or later, sin will cause sorrow; and those who will not repent of their sin, may justly be left to pine away in it. There are many whose wealth is their snare and ruin; and the gaining the world is the losing of their souls. Riches profit not in the day of wrath. The wealth of this world has not that in it which will answer the desires of the soul, or be any satisfaction to it in a day of distress. God's temple shall stand them in no stead. Those are unworthy to be honoured with the form of godliness, who will not be governed by its power.
7.m: Sin always leads to sadness eventually. People who won’t stop sinning might just be left to suffer because of it. Many people find that having lots of money causes more trouble than help. Gaining everything in the world often means losing your soul. Money doesn’t help on the day you face judgment. This world’s riches can’t satisfy your deepest needs or bring comfort during hard times. Having a religious appearance isn’t enough if you don’t live according to true faith.

7.o: Whoever break the bands of God's law, will find themselves bound and held by the chains of his judgments. Since they encouraged one another to sin, God would dishearten them. All must needs be in trouble, when God comes to judge them according to their deserts. May the Lord enable us to seek that good part which shall not be taken away.
7.m: Anyone who breaks God's laws will end up trapped by his punishments. Because they cheered each other on to do wrong, God wants to discourage them. Everyone will face problems when God judges them fairly. Let’s hope the Lord helps us choose what’s right so we won’t lose our blessings.

8.o: The glorious personage Ezekiel beheld in vision, seemed to take hold upon him, and he was conveyed in spirit to Jerusalem. There, in the inner court of the temple, was prepared a place for some base idol. The whole was presented in vision to the prophet. If it should please God to give any man a clear view of his glory and majesty, and of all the abominations committing in any one city, he would then admit the justice of the severest punishments God should inflict thereon.
8.m:
Ezekiel saw a bright figure in a vision who took hold of him. He was taken in spirit to Jerusalem. In the inner courtyard of the temple, Ezekiel saw a spot prepared for an ugly idol. 

If God showed someone clearly how glorious and majestic he is, and all the bad things happening in a city, that person would agree that God’s harshest punishments on that city were justified.

8.o: A secret place was, as it were, opened, where the prophet saw creatures painted on the walls, and a number of the elders of Israel worshipped before them. No superiority in worldly matters will preserve men from lust, or idolatries, when they are left to their own deceitful hearts; and those who are soon wearied in the service of God, often grudge no toil nor expense when following their superstitions. When hypocrites screen themselves behind the wall of an outward profession, there is some hole or other left in the wall, something that betrays them to those who look diligently. There is a great deal of secret wickedness in the world. They think themselves out of God's sight. But those are ripe indeed for ruin, who lay the blame of their sins upon the Lord.
8.m:
A hidden place was shown to the prophet where he saw pictures of animals on the walls, and many leaders of Israel were worshipping these images. Having power or wealth won't stop people from being tempted by sin or false gods if they listen only to their own desires. People who get tired quickly of serving God often put in lots of effort and spend money on their wrong beliefs. 

When fake believers hide behind a pretend devotion to God, there’s usually something that gives them away to those who pay close attention. There is much hidden evil in the world. These people think they’re out of God’s view. But those who blame God for their sins are ready for punishment.

8.o: The yearly lamenting for Tammuz was attended with infamous practices; and the worshippers of the sun here described, are supposed to have been priests. The Lord appeals to the prophet concerning the heinousness of the crime; "and lo, they put the branch to their nose," denoting some custom used by idolaters in honour of the idols they served. The more we examine human nature and our own hearts, the more abominations we shall discover; and the longer the believer searches himself, the more he will humble himself before God, and the more will he value the fountain open for sin, and seek to wash therein.
8.m:
The yearly mourning for Tammuz included bad practices. People thought these were priests who worshiped the sun. The Lord asks the prophet about how serious the wrongdoing was. "And look, they put the branch to their nose." This shows a custom that people did to honor their idols.

As we look closely at human nature and our own hearts, we find many wrong things. The longer a believer looks inside themselves, the more they will feel sorry and humble before God. They will also see how much they need to use the open fountain for washing away their sins.

9.o: It is a great comfort to believers, that in the midst of destroyers and destructions, there is a Mediator, a great High Priest, who has an interest in heaven, and in whom saints on earth have an interest. The representation of the Divine glory from above the ark, removed to the threshold, denoted that the Lord was about to leave his mercy-seat, and to pronounce judgment on the people. The distinguishing character of this remnant that is to be saved, is such as sigh and cry to God in prayer, because of the abominations in Jerusalem. Those who keep pure in times of general wickedness, God will keep safe in times of general trouble and distress.
9.m:
Believers can find comfort knowing there is a Mediator, a great High Priest, who represents them in heaven. When the glory over the ark moved to the entrance, it showed that the Lord was getting ready to leave the mercy seat and judge the people. 

The special trait of those who will be saved is that they pray to God, crying out because of the sins in Jerusalem. People who stay pure during times of widespread evil, God will protect during times of trouble and danger.

9.o: The slaughter must begin at the sanctuary, that all may see and know that the Lord hates sin most in those nearest to him. He who was appointed to protect, reported the matter. Christ is faithful to the trust reposed in him. Is he commanded by his Father to secure eternal life to the chosen remnant? He says, Of all that thou hast given me, I have lost none. If others perish, and we are saved, we must ascribe the difference wholly to the mercy of our God, for we too have deserved wrath. Let us still continue to plead in behalf of others. But where the Lord shows no mercy he does no injustice; he only recompenses men's ways.
9.m: The killing must start at the sanctuary so everyone can see that the Lord hates sin the most in those closest to him. The one chosen to protect reported what happened. Jesus is faithful with the task given to him. When told by his Father to save the chosen people forever, he says, “Of all you gave me, I have lost none.” If others are lost while we are saved, we must give full credit to God’s mercy because we deserve punishment just as much. We should keep asking God to show mercy on others. But when God doesn’t show mercy, he isn’t being unfair; he simply gives people what they deserve.

10.o: The fire being taken from between the wheels, under the cherubim, Ezek. 1:13, seems to have signified the wrath of God to be executed upon Jerusalem. It intimated that the fire of Divine wrath, which kindles judgment upon a people, is just and holy; and in the great day, the earth, and all the works that are therein, will be burnt up.
10.m: The fire coming from between the wheels, under the cherubim, in Ezekiel 1:13, seems to show that God’s anger would be poured out on Jerusalem. This tells us that the fire of God’s anger, which brings judgment on a people, is right and pure. And one day, everything on Earth, including all the things we see around us, will be burned up.

10.o: Ezekiel sees the working of Divine providence in the government of the lower world, and the affairs of it. When God is leaving a people in displeasure, angels above, and all events below, further his departure. The Spirit of life, the Spirit of God, directs all creatures, in heaven and on earth, so as to make them serve the Divine purpose. God removes by degrees from a provoking people; and, when ready to depart, would return to them, if they were a repenting, praying people. Let this warn sinners to seek the Lord while he may be found, and to call on him while he is near, and cause us all to walk humbly and watchfully with our God.
10.m:
Ezekiel saw how God controls everything in the world. When God is upset with a group of people, both angels in heaven and things happening on Earth help carry out His plan. 

The Holy Spirit guides all living beings, in heaven and on Earth, to do what God wants. God slowly pulls away from people who keep disobeying Him. But if those people start asking for forgiveness and pray, He might come back to them.

This should remind us to turn to God while we still have time. We should ask for His help before it’s too late. It shows us to live carefully and stay close to God at all times.

11.o: Where Satan cannot persuade men to look upon the judgment to come as uncertain, he gains his point by persuading them to look upon it as at a distance. These wretched rulers dare to say, We are as safe in this city as flesh in a boiling pot; the walls of the city shall be to us as walls of brass, we shall receive no more damage from the besiegers than the caldron does from the fire. When sinners flatter themselves to their own ruin, it is time to tell them they shall have no peace if they go on. None shall remain in possession of the city but those who are buried in it. Those are least safe who are most secure. God is often pleased to single out some sinners for warning to others. Whether Pelatiah died at that time in Jerusalem, or when the fulfilment of the prophecy drew near, is uncertain. Like Ezekiel, we ought to be much affected with the sudden death of others, and we should still plead with the Lord to have mercy on those who remain.
11.m: Where Satan can't convince people that judgment might not happen, he convinces them that it will happen far in the future. These bad leaders said they were just as safe in the city as meat in a cooking pot. They thought the city’s walls would protect them like walls made of brass, keeping them safe from attackers. Sinners who fool themselves about their safety need to hear that trouble is coming if they don’t change. Only dead bodies will stay inside the city. The safest people aren’t the ones who think they’re safe. Sometimes God uses specific sinners to warn others. It’s unclear whether Pelatiah died then in Jerusalem or later when the prediction came true. Like Ezekiel, we should feel sad when someone dies suddenly and ask God to show mercy to those left behind.

11.o: The pious captives in Babylon were insulted by the Jews who continued in Jerusalem; but God made gracious promises to them. It is promised, that God will give them one heart; a heart firmly fixed for God, and not wavering. All who are made holy have a new spirit, a new temper and dispositions; they act from new principles, walk by new rules, and aim at new ends. A new name, or a new face, will not serve without a new spirit. If any man be in Christ, he is a new creature. The carnal heart, like a stone, cannot be made to feel. Men live among the dead and dying, and are neither concerned nor humbled. He will make their hearts tender and fit to receive impressions: this is God's work, it is his gift by promise; and a wonderful and happy change is wrought by it, from death to life. Their practices shall be agreeable to those principles. These two must and will go together. When the sinner feels his need of these blessings, let him present the promises as prayers in the name of Christ, they will be performed.
11.m:
The good people who were taken captive to Babylon faced insults from the Jews who stayed in Jerusalem. But God promised to bless them.

God said he would give them one heart—a strong, steady heart focused on Him. People who become holy get a new spirit, with fresh thoughts and feelings. They follow different rules now and have new goals. Just changing our appearance won’t do anything if we don't change inside too. 

If someone belongs to Christ, they become a new person. A hardhearted soul, like a rock, doesn't feel much. Many people live around others who are sick or dying but aren’t bothered by it. 

But God will soften their hearts so they can truly care about what He wants. This is God’s work, His special gift. It’s amazing how He changes us from being dead spiritually to alive. 

Their actions will match their new beliefs. These two things always go hand in hand. When sinners realize they need these gifts, they should ask God for them using Jesus' name. Then God will surely grant these requests.

11.o: Here is the departure of God's presence from the city and temple. It was from the Mount of Olives that the vision went up, typifying the ascension of Christ to heaven from that very mountain. Though the Lord will not forsake his people, yet he may be driven away from any part of his visible church by their sins, and woe will be upon them when He withdraws his presence, glory, and protection.
11.m: Here is where God's presence leaves the city and the temple. The vision went up from the Mount of Olives, which shows us that Jesus went to heaven from that same mountain. Even though God won't abandon his people, he might still leave parts of his church because of their sins. When he goes, there will be trouble for those who are left behind.

12.o: By the preparation for removal, and his breaking through the wall of his house at evening, as one desirous to escape from the enemy, the prophet signified the conduct and fate of Zedekiah. When God has delivered us, we must glorify him and edify others, by acknowledging our sins. Those who by afflictions are brought to this, are made to know that God is the Lord, and may help to bring others to know him.
12.m:
The prophet showed what would happen to Zedekiah by getting ready to leave and breaking through the wall of his house at night to try to escape from the enemy. 

When God saves us, we should honor him and help others by admitting our mistakes. People who learn about God because of their troubles can teach others too.

12.o: The prophet must eat and drink in care and fear, with trembling, that he might express the condition of those in Jerusalem during the siege. When ministers speak of the ruin coming upon sinners, they must speak as those that know the terrors of the Lord. Afflictions are happy ones, however grievous to flesh and blood, that improve us in the knowledge of God.
12.m: The prophet should eat and drink while feeling worried and afraid, showing concern for people in Jerusalem during the siege. When preachers talk about the punishment coming to sinners, they need to speak as if they understand the fearsome power of God. Even though troubles may be hard on our bodies, they can help us learn more about God.

12.o: From that forbearance of God, which should have led them to repent, the Jews hardened themselves in sin. It will not serve for an excuse in speaking evil, to plead that it is a common saying. There is but a step between us and an awful eternity; therefore it concerns us to get ready for a future state. No one will be able to put from himself the evil day, unless by seeking peace with the Lord.
12.m: Because of God’s patience, the Jews became more stubborn in their sins. We cannot use the fact that others say something bad about someone else as an excuse to do the same. Life can change quickly, so we need to prepare for what comes after. The only way to avoid facing judgment is by making peace with the Lord.

13.o: Where God gives a warrant to do any thing, he gives wisdom. What they delivered was not what they had seen or heard, as that is which the ministers of Christ deliver. They were not praying prophets, had no intercourse with Heaven; they contrived how to please people, not how to do them good; they stood not against sin. They flattered people into vain hopes. Such widen the breach, by causing men to think themselves deserving of eternal life, when the wrath of God abides upon them.
13.m:
When God allows us to do something, he gives us the wisdom to know how to do it well. 

What these people taught wasn’t based on anything they saw or heard, unlike what Christian ministers teach. 

They weren’t praying prophets who talked to heaven. Instead, they thought about ways to please people rather than help them. 

They didn’t stand up against sin. 

Instead, they told people false hopes. 

This makes the gap wider because it leads people to believe they deserve eternal life even though God’s anger still rests on them.

13.o: One false prophet built the wall, set up the notion that Jerusalem should be victorious, and made himself acceptable by it. Others made the matter yet more plausible and promising; they daubed the wall which the first had built; but they would, ere long, be undeceived when their work was beaten down by the storm of God's just wrath; when the Chaldean army desolated the land. Hopes of peace and happiness, not warranted by the word of God, will cheat men; like a wall well daubed, but ill built.
13.m: A false prophet said that Jerusalem would win and became popular because of it. Other people added to his ideas, making them seem even better. But soon enough, their hopes would be destroyed. The walls they built would be knocked down by God’s punishment through the Chaldean army. False promises of peace and happiness, not supported by God’s Word, are like walls that look good on the outside but aren’t strong inside.

13.o: It is ill with those who had rather hear pleasing lies than unpleasing truths. The false prophetesses tried to make people secure, signified by laying them at ease, and to make them proud, signified by the finery laid on their heads. They shall be confounded in their attempts, and God's people shall be delivered out of their hands. It behoves Christians to keep close to the word of God, and in every thing to seek the teaching of the Holy Spirit. Let us so trust the promises of God as to keep his commandments.
13.m:
Those who prefer hearing nice lies over uncomfortable truths are in trouble. False prophets tried to comfort people by making them feel safe and confident. They did this by putting them at ease and dressing them up nicely. But their efforts will fail, and God’s people will be freed from their control. 

Christians should stay true to what the Bible says and always ask for guidance from the Holy Spirit. We should believe God’s promises enough to follow his commands.

14.o: No outward form or reformation can be acceptable to God, so long as any idol possesses the heart; yet how many prefer their own devices and their own righteousness, to the way of salvation! Men's corruptions are idols in their hearts, and are of their own setting up; God will let them take their course. Sin renders the sinner odious in the eyes of the pure and holy God; and in his own eyes also, whenever conscience is awakened. Let us seek to be cleansed from the guilt and pollution of sins, in that fountain which the Lord has opened.
14.m:
Outward changes and reforms cannot please God if an idol still rules the heart. Many people choose their own ways over God’s path to salvation. The wrong things we set up in our hearts act like idols. They are our own doing. God lets these idols stay there.

Sin makes sinners hateful to the perfect and holy God. It also makes us hate ourselves when our consciences wake up. We should ask God to clean us from the guilt and dirt of our sins through the cleansing he provides.

14.o: National sins bring national judgments. Though sinners escape one judgment, another is waiting for them. When God's professing people rebel against him, they may justly expect all his judgments. The faith, obedience, and prayers of Noah prevailed to the saving of his house, but not of the old world. Job's sacrifice and prayer in behalf of his friends were accepted, and Daniel had prevailed for the saving his companions and the wise men of Babylon. But a people that had filled the measure of their sins, was not to expect to escape for the sake of any righteous men living among them; not even of the most eminent saints, who could be accepted in their own case only through the sufferings and righteousness of Christ. Yet even when God makes the greatest desolations by his judgments, he saves some to be monuments of his mercy. In firm belief that we shall approve the whole of God's dealings with ourselves, and with all mankind, let us silence all rebellious murmurs and objections.
14.m:
National sins lead to national punishments. Even if sinners avoid one punishment, another will come. When God’s people turn away from Him, they should expect His judgments. 

Noah’s faith, obedience, and prayers saved his family, but not the rest of the world. Job’s sacrifices and prayers helped save his friends, and Daniel prayed to save his companions and the wise men of Babylon. However, a nation full of sin cannot hope to be spared because of a few good people, even the best ones. They need to rely on Jesus’ suffering and righteousness.

Even during times of great destruction, God still saves some people as reminders of His mercy. We should trust that God’s actions towards everyone, including us, are always right. This means we shouldn’t complain about what He does.

16.o: In this chapter God's dealings with the Jewish nation, and their conduct towards him, are described, and their punishment through the surrounding nations, even those they most trusted in. This is done under the parable of an exposed infant rescued from death, educated, espoused, and richly provided for, but afterwards guilty of the most abandoned conduct, and punished for it; yet at last received into favour, and ashamed of her base conduct. We are not to judge of these expressions by modern ideas, but by those of the times and places in which they were used, where many of them would not sound as they do to us. The design was to raise hatred to idolatry, and such a parable was well suited for that purpose.
16.m:
In this chapter, God’s treatment of the Jewish people and their behavior toward Him is explained. It describes how they were punished by other countries, including ones they trusted the most. 

This story uses the image of a baby left outside who was saved from danger, raised, married, and given everything she needed. But then she did very bad things and got punished for it. In the end, she was forgiven and felt sorry for her actions.

We shouldn’t think about these words using today’s ideas, but rather how people back then understood them. Some parts might seem strange to us now. The goal was to show how much God hates idol worship, and telling the story this way worked well for that purpose.

16.o: After a full warning of judgments, mercy is remembered, mercy is reserved. These closing verses are a precious promise, in part fulfilled at the return of the penitent and reformed Jews out of Babylon, but to have fuller accomplishment in gospel times. The Divine mercy should be powerful to melt our hearts into godly sorrow for sin. Nor will God ever leave the sinner to perish, who is humbled for his sins, and comes to trust in His mercy and grace through Jesus Christ; but will keep him by his power, through faith unto salvation.
16.m:
After giving warnings about punishments, God remembers mercy. These final verses are a valuable promise. It was partly fulfilled when the repentant and reformed Jews returned from Babylon. But it will be fully completed in the time of the gospel.

God’s mercy should soften our hearts and cause us to feel sorry for our sins. God will never let a person who feels bad about their sins and trusts in His mercy through Jesus Christ be destroyed. Instead, He will protect them through faith until they reach salvation.

17.o: Mighty conquerors are aptly likened to birds or beasts of prey, but their destructive passions are overruled to forward God's designs. Those who depart from God, only vary their crimes by changing one carnal confidence for another, and never will prosper.
17.m: Powerful leaders are often compared to birds or animals that hunt other creatures. But even their harmful desires can be used by God to achieve his purposes. People who turn away from God just replace one selfish trust with another and never succeed.

17.o: The parable is explained, and the particulars of the history of the Jewish nation at that time may be traced. Zedekiah had been ungrateful to his benefactor, which is a sin against God. In every solemn oath, God is appealed to as a witness of the sincerity of him that swears. Truth is a debt owing to all men. If the professors of the true religion deal treacherously with those of a false religion, their profession makes their sin the worse; and God will the more surely and severely punish it. The Lord will not hold those guiltless who take his name in vain; and no man shall escape the righteous judgment of God who dies under unrepented guilt.
17.m:
This story explains what was happening back then with the Jewish people. King Zedekiah was unthankful to someone who helped him, which is a sin against God. When people swear an oath, they ask God to be a witness that they're telling the truth. We owe honesty to everyone. 

If believers in the true religion cheat those who follow other religions, their faith makes them even more guilty. And God will punish them more for it. God won't let anyone get away with taking His name in vain. Nobody will avoid being judged fairly by God if they die without repenting.

17.o: The unbelief of man shall not make the promise of God of none effect. The parable of a tree, used in the threatening, is here presented in the promise. It appears only applicable to Jesus, the Son of David, the Messiah of God. The kingdom of Satan, which has borne so long, so large a sway, shall be broken, and the kingdom of Christ, which was looked upon with contempt, shall be established. Blessed be God, our Redeemer is seen even by the ends of the earth. We may find refuge from the wrath to come, and from every enemy and danger, under his shadow; and believers are fruitful in him.
17.m:
Man’s doubt won’t stop God’s promises from coming true. 

In the warning, there’s a story about a tree. This story fits perfectly with Jesus, who is the Son of David and the Messiah. 

Satan’s rule will end, and the kingdom of Christ, which people once thought would fail, will become strong. 

Thankfully, Jesus, our Savior, is known all over the world. 

We can find safety from God’s judgment and every threat under Jesus’ protection. And those who believe in him grow and produce much fruit.

18.o: The soul that sinneth it shall die. As to eternity, every man was, is, and will be dealt with, as his conduct shows him to have been under the old covenant of works, or the new covenant of grace. Whatever outward sufferings come upon men through the sins of others, they deserve for their own sins all they suffer; and the Lord overrules every event for the eternal good of believers. All souls are in the hand of the great Creator: he will deal with them in justice or mercy; nor will any perish for the sins of another, who is not in some sense worthy of death for his own. We all have sinned, and our souls must be lost, if God deal with us according to his holy law; but we are invited to come to Christ. If a man who had shown his faith by his works, had a wicked son, whose character and conduct were the reverse of his parent's, could it be expected he should escape the Divine vengeance on account of his father's piety? Surely not. And should a wicked man have a son who walked before God as righteous, this man would not perish for his father's sins. If the son was not free from evils in this life, still he should be partaker of salvation. The question here is not about the meritorious ground of justification, but about the Lord's dealings with the righteous and the wicked.
18.m:
The soul that sins will die. When it comes to eternity, everyone is treated based on how they lived, whether under the old rules or the new ones of grace. People suffer for their own sins, even if those sufferings come because of what other people did wrong. God uses everything that happens to teach lessons about what’s best for those who believe. 

All souls belong to the great Creator, who treats each one fairly. No one will be punished forever just because someone else did wrong. We’ve all done things wrong, and without help, our souls would be lost. But God invites us to turn to Jesus for forgiveness.

If a faithful person had an evil child, could the child avoid punishment just because the parent was good? Of course not. Likewise, if a bad person had a godly child, the parent wouldn’t be saved just because the child was good. Even if the godly child faced troubles, they would still receive salvation.

This isn't about earning salvation through good deeds. It’s about how God deals differently with those who are righteous and those who aren’t.

18.o: The wicked man would be saved, if he turned from his evil ways. The true penitent is a true believer. None of his former transgressions shall be mentioned unto him, but in the righteousness which he has done, as the fruit of faith and the effect of conversion, he shall surely live. The question is not whether the truly righteous ever become apostates. It is certain that many who for a time were thought to be righteous, do so, while Ezek. 18:26,27 speaks the fulness of pardoning mercy: when sin is forgiven, it is blotted out, it is remembered no more. In their righteousness they shall live; not for their righteousness, as if that were an atonement for their sins, but in their righteousness, which is one of the blessings purchased by the Mediator. What encouragement a repenting, returning sinner has to hope for pardon and life according to this promise! In verse 28 is the beginning and progress of repentance. True believers watch and pray, and continue to the end, and they are saved. In all our disputes with God, he is in the right, and we are in the wrong.
18.m:
A wicked person could be saved if they changed their ways. A true penitent is someone who truly believes. Their past mistakes will not be held against them. Instead, what matters is the good they do because of their faith and turning away from sin. 

The question isn't about whether those who are truly righteous might turn away later. Many people who seemed righteous at first sometimes do. But Ezekiel 18:26,27 tells us that when God forgives sins, He forgets them completely. People will live based on their goodness, not because their goodness makes up for their sins, but because their goodness shows they have been forgiven. This gives great hope to anyone who repents and returns to God.

Verse 28 talks about starting and growing in true repentance. Those who believe sincerely keep watching, praying, and staying faithful until the end. When we argue with God, we are always wrong and He is always right.

18.o: The Lord will judge each of the Israelites according to his ways. On this is grounded an exhortation to repent, and to make them a new heart and a new spirit. God does not command what cannot be done, but admonishes us to do what is in our power, and to pray for what is not. Ordinances and means are appointed, directions and promises are given, that those who desire this change may seek it from God.
18.m: The Lord will judge each Israelite based on their actions. This leads to a call to change and become better people. God doesn't ask us to do things we can't do. Instead, he tells us to do what we can and to pray for help with the rest. He gives us rules, guidance, and promises so that anyone who wants to change can ask him for help.

19.o: Ezekiel is to compare the kingdom of Judah to a lioness. He must compare the kings of Judah to a lion's whelps; they were cruel and oppressive to their own subjects. The righteousness of God is to be acknowledged, when those who have terrified and enslaved others, are themselves terrified and enslaved. When professors of religion form connexions with ungodly persons, their children usually grow up following after the maxims and fashions of a wicked world. Advancement to authority discovers the ambition and selfishness of men's hearts; and those who spend their lives in mischief, generally end them by violence.
19.m:
Ezekiel should compare the kingdom of Judah to a lioness. He needs to see the kings of Judah as cubs from a lion. These kings were mean and harsh to their people.

We need to recognize God’s justice when those who scared and controlled others are now scared and controlled themselves. 

When religious leaders connect with bad people, their kids often follow those bad habits and ways.

Getting power shows how greedy and self-centered people really are. Those who cause trouble all their lives usually die violently.

19.o: Jerusalem was a vine, flourishing and fruitful. This vine is now destroyed, though not plucked up by the roots. She has by wickedness made herself like tinder to the sparks of God's wrath, so that her own branches serve as fuel to burn her. Blessed be God, one Branch of the vine here alluded to, is not only become a strong rod for the sceptre of those that rule, but is Himself the true and living Vine. This shall be for a rejoicing to all the chosen people of God throughout all generations.
19.m:
Jerusalem was once a healthy, productive vine. Now she is destroyed, even though the roots still remain. Her wickedness has made her like kindling, ready to catch fire from God’s anger. So her own parts help burn her down. 

But praise God, there is one branch that stands out. It became a strong ruler, and more than that, He is the real, living Vine. This will bring joy to God’s chosen people for all time.

20.o: The Jews persisted in rebellion after they settled in the land of Canaan. And these elders seem to have thought of uniting with the heathen. We make nothing by our profession if it be but a profession. There is nothing got by sinful compliances; and the carnal projects of hypocrites will stand them in no stead.
20.m: After settling in the land of Canaan, the Jews continued to rebel. Some of their leaders seemed to want to join forces with people who didn’t believe in God. Having faith means more than just saying you do. Sinful actions don’t help anyone. Hypocrites can't rely on their selfish plans.

20.o: The wicked Israelites, notwithstanding they follow the sinful ways of other nations, shall not mingle with them in their prosperity, but shall be separated from them for destruction. There is no shaking off God's dominion; and those who will not yield to the power of his grace, shall sink under the power of his wrath. But not one of God's jewels shall be lost in the lumber of this world. He will bring the jews to the land of Israel again; and will give them true repentance. They will be overcome with his kindness: the more we know of God's holiness, the more we see the hateful nature of sin. Those who remain unaffected amidst means of grace, and would live without Christ, like the world around them, may be sure it is the way to destruction.
20.m:
The wicked Israelites will not share in the good fortune of other nations even though they copy their sinful behaviors. Instead, God will separate them for punishment. It’s impossible to escape God’s rule. People who won’t accept His mercy through grace will face His anger instead. 

But none of God’s chosen people will be lost among the rest of the world. God will bring His people back to Israel and grant them true regret for their sins. Their hearts will soften toward Him because of His goodness. The more we learn about God’s purity, the clearer it becomes how much we hate sin.

Those who ignore God’s teachings and try to live like the world without Jesus are heading towards ruin.

20.o: Judah and Jerusalem had been full of people, as a forest of trees, but empty of fruit. God's word prophesies against those who bring not forth the fruits of righteousness. When He will ruin a nation, who or what can save it? The plainest truths were as parables to the people. It is common for those who will not be wrought upon by the word, to blame it.
20.m: Judah and Jerusalem used to have lots of people, like a forest has many trees. But now there are no good results from all these people. God’s message warns about those who do not produce good deeds. When God decides to punish a country, nothing can stop it. Simple truths seemed like riddles to the people. Those who refuse to listen to God’s words often criticize them.

21.o: Here is an explanation of the parable in the last chapter. It is declared that the Lord was about to cut off Jerusalem and the whole land, that all might know it was his decree against a wicked and rebellious people. It behoves those who denounce the awful wrath of God against sinners, to show that they do not desire the woful day. The example of Christ teaches us to lament over those whose ruin we declare. Whatever instruments God uses in executing his judgments, he will strengthen them according to the service they are employed in. The sword glitters to the terror of those against whom it is drawn. It is a sword to others, a rod to the people of the Lord. God is in earnest in pronouncing this sentence, and the prophet must show himself in earnest in publishing it.
21.m:
Here is an explanation of the parable from the previous chapter. The Lord was going to punish Jerusalem and the entire land because of their wickedness and rebellion. When telling others about God’s anger towards sinners, it’s important to show that you don’t want bad things to happen to them. Jesus taught us to feel sad for those we warn about judgment. 

Whatever tools God uses to carry out his punishments, he makes sure they work well. The sword frightens those it is aimed at. To others, it is a weapon. To God’s people, it is a warning stick. God means business when he speaks these words, and the prophet must be serious when sharing them.

21.o: By the Spirit of prophecy Ezekiel foresaw Nebuchadnezzar's march from Babylon, which he would determine by divination. The Lord would overturn the government of Judah, till the coming of Him whose right it is. This seems to foretell the overturnings of the Jewish nation to the present day, and the troubles of states and kingdoms, which shall make way for establishing the Messiah's kingdom throughout the earth. The Lord secretly leads all to adopt his wise designs. And in the midst of the most tremendous warnings of wrath, we still hear of mercy, and some mention of Him through whom mercy is shown to sinful men.
21.m:
Ezekiel saw ahead of time, using God’s guidance, that King Nebuchadnezzar would lead an army out of Babylon. God planned to change the leadership in Judah until the one who truly has the right to rule comes. 

This might be talking about all the times the Jewish people faced trouble up to now. It could also mean that nations will face problems that pave the way for Jesus’ kingdom to spread everywhere.

God quietly guides everyone to do what fits his plans. Even in messages warning about punishment, there is always talk of mercy and Jesus, who brings forgiveness to those who have done wrong.

21.o: The diviners of the Ammonites made false prophecies of victory. They would never recover their power, but in time would be wholly forgotten. Let us be thankful to be employed as instruments of mercy; let us use our understandings in doing good; and let us stand aloof from men who are only skilful to destroy.
21.m: The fortune-tellers of Ammon gave false predictions of winning. They would never regain their strength and would eventually be completely forgotten. We should be grateful to do kind things for others. Let us use our minds to help people. And we should stay away from those who are skilled only at causing harm.

22.o: The prophet is to judge the bloody city; the city of bloods. Jerusalem is so called, because of her crimes. The sins which Jerusalem stands charged with, are exceeding sinful. Murder, idolatry, disobedience to parents, oppression and extortion, profanation of the sabbath and holy things, seventh commandment sins, lewdness and adultery. Unmindfulness of God was at the bottom of all this wickedness. Sinners provoke God because they forget him. Jerusalem has filled the measure of her sins. Those who give up themselves to be ruled by their lusts, will justly be given up to be portioned by them. Those who resolve to be their own masters, let them expect no other happiness than their own hands can furnish; and a miserable portion it will prove.
22.m:
The prophet is to judge the violent city; the city full of violence. Jerusalem is called this because of her crimes. 

Jerusalem’s sins include murder, worshiping idols, disobeying parents, being unfair and greedy, treating the Sabbath and holy things badly, breaking the laws about sex, and being immoral and unfaithful.

People in Jerusalem forgot about God, which led to all these bad actions. When people ignore God, they make him angry.

Jerusalem has done enough wrong to deserve punishment. People who let their desires control them will get what they deserve. If someone decides to live however they want, they should expect only the happiness they create for themselves, which won’t be very good.

22.o: Israel, compared with other nations, had been as the gold and silver compared with baser metals. But they were now as the refuse that is consumed in the furnace, or thrown away when the silver is refined. Sinners, especially backsliding professors, are, in God's account, useless and fit for nothing. When God brings his own people into the furnace, he sits by them as the refiner by his gold, to see that they are not continued there any longer than is fitting and needful. The dross shall be wholly separated, and the good metal purified. Let those who suffer pains, or lingering sickness, and find that their hearts can scarcely bear these light and momentary afflictions, take warning to flee from the wrath to come; for if these trials are not sanctified by the power of the Holy Spirit, to the cleansing their hearts and hands from sin, far worse things will come upon them.
22.m:
Israel was once like gold and silver compared to other nations, which were like less valuable metals. Now Israel is like waste left behind after refining precious metals. 

Sinners, especially those who claim faith but turn away, become useless in God’s eyes. 

When God puts His people through hard times, He watches closely to ensure they stay there only as long as needed. All impurities will be removed, leaving only pure goodness.

Those suffering pain or enduring long illnesses should pay attention. If their struggles aren’t used by the Holy Spirit to clean their hearts and lives of sin, even worse troubles await them.

22.o: All orders and degrees of men had helped to fill the measure of the nation's guilt. The people that had any power abused it, and even the buyers and sellers find some way to oppress one another. It bodes ill to a people when judgments are breaking in upon them, and the spirit of prayer is restrained. Let all who fear God, unite to promote his truth and righteousness; as wicked men of every rank and profession plot together to run them down.
22.m: Everyone who had power did wrong things and added to the country’s guilt. People in charge used their authority badly, and even regular buyers and sellers found ways to cheat each other. When bad things start happening to a people and they lose the desire to pray, that is a sign of trouble. Those who respect God should work together to support what is right and true. Wicked people from all walks of life team up to try to stop good things.

24.o: The pot on the fire represented Jerusalem besieged by the Chaldeans: all orders and ranks were within the walls, prepared as a prey for the enemy. They ought to have put away their transgressions, as the scum, which rises by the heat of the fire, is taken from the top of the pot. But they grew worse, and their miseries increased. Jerusalem was to be levelled with the ground. The time appointed for the punishment of wicked men may seem to come slowly, but it will come surely. It is sad to think how many there are, on whom ordinances and providences are all lost.
24.m:
The pot on the fire stood for Jerusalem under siege by the Chaldeans. Everyone inside the city walls was ready to be captured by the enemy. They should have gotten rid of their sins, just like scum that floats to the top of boiling water gets skimmed off. Instead, they became even more sinful, and their troubles got worse. 

Jerusalem would eventually be destroyed completely. The day of judgment might take a long time to arrive, but it will definitely happen. It’s sad to see so many people who don’t learn from God’s teachings and events around them.

24.o: Though mourning for the dead is a duty, yet it must be kept under by religion and right reason: we must not sorrow as men that have no hope. Believers must not copy the language and expressions of those who know not God. The people asked the meaning of the sign. God takes from them all that was dearest to them. And as Ezekiel wept not for his affliction, so neither should they weep for theirs. Blessed be God, we need not pine away under our afflictions; for should all comforts fail, and all sorrows be united, yet the broken heart and the mourner's prayer are always acceptable before God.
24.m:
Even though it’s important to mourn for the dead, believers shouldn’t let their sadness take over. They should keep it in check because they have hope. 

Believers shouldn’t use the same words as people who don’t know God. When the people asked what the sign meant, God took away everything dear to them. Just as Ezekiel didn’t cry about his troubles, they shouldn’t cry about theirs either.

Thankfully, we don’t have to feel hopeless during hard times. Even if all other comfort goes away, God still accepts the prayers of someone who is truly sad and brokenhearted.

25.o: Though one event seem to the righteous and wicked, it is vastly different. Those who glory in any other defence and protection than the Divine power, providence, and promise, will, sooner or later, be ashamed of their glorying. Those who will not leave it to God to take vengeance for them, may expect that he will take vengeance on them. The equity of the Lord's judgments is to be observed, when he not only avenges injuries upon those that did them, but by those against whom they were done. Those who treasure up old hatred, and watch for the opportunity of manifesting it, are treasuring up for themselves wrath against the day of wrath.
25.m:
Though an event might look the same to both good people and bad people, it means very different things. People who trust in anything besides God’s power, care, and promises will eventually feel embarrassed about what they trusted in. 

If someone won’t let God handle revenge for them, they should expect God to punish them instead. When God punishes wrongdoers, He shows fairness by punishing those who caused harm and even those who didn’t protect themselves properly. 

People who hold onto old grudges and wait for chances to show anger are storing up trouble for themselves. They’ll face God’s judgment someday.

26.o: To be secretly pleased with the death or decay of others, when we are likely to get by it; or with their fall, when we may thrive upon it, is a sin that easily besets us, yet is not thought so bad as really it is. But it comes from a selfish, covetous principle, and from that love of the world as our happiness, which the love of God expressly forbids. He often blasts the projects of those who would raise themselves on the ruin of others. The maxims most current in the trading world, are directly opposed to the law of God. But he will show himself against the money-loving, selfish traders, whose hearts, like those of Tyre, are hardened by the love of riches. Men have little cause to glory in things which stir up the envy and rapacity of others, and which are continually shifting from one to another; and in getting, keeping, and spending which, men provoke that God whose wrath turns joyous cities into ruinous heaps.
26.m:
Being happy about someone else’s problems if you think you’ll benefit is wrong. People don’t always see how bad this is. It comes from being too focused on yourself and loving money more than God.

God stops people from succeeding if they try to rise by hurting others. 

The rules in business often go against what God wants. 

He will punish greedy traders who care only about making money, just like the people of Tyre did.

People shouldn’t brag about possessions that make others jealous and keep changing hands. Gaining, holding onto, and using these things makes God angry. His anger can turn joyful cities into ruins.

26.o: See how high, how great Tyre had been. See how low Tyre is made. The fall of others should awaken us out of security. Every discovery of the fulfilment of a Scripture prophecy, is like a miracle to confirm our faith. All that is earthly is vanity and vexation. Those who now have the most established prosperity, will soon be out of sight and forgotten.
26.m: Notice how powerful Tyre once was. Now see how low it has fallen. When we hear about other people’s falls, it should wake us up from feeling too secure. Each time a prophecy comes true, it strengthens our belief. Everything in this world is empty and meaningless. People who seem very successful today will soon be forgotten.

27.o: Those who live at ease are to be lamented, if they are not prepared for trouble. Let none reckon themselves beautified, any further than they are sanctified. The account of the trade of Tyre intimates, that God's eye is upon men when employed in worldly business. Not only when at church, praying and hearing, but when in markets and fairs, buying and selling. In all our dealings we should keep a conscience void of offence. God, as the common Father of mankind, makes one country abound in one commodity, and another in another, serviceable to the necessity or to the comfort and ornament of human life. See what a blessing trade and merchandise are to mankind, when followed in the fear of God. Besides necessaries, an abundance of things are made valuable only by custom; yet God allows us to use them. But when riches increase, men are apt to set their hearts upon them, and forget the Lord, who gives power to get wealth.
27.m:
People who live comfortably need to prepare for hard times. Those who think they look good because of their appearance aren’t really beautiful unless they’re holy too.

The story about the city of Tyre shows that God watches people even when they're doing regular work. He sees them whether they’re in church praying or out shopping.

We should always try to do the right thing in everything we do, whether we’re at home or at work. 

God, as everyone’s father, helps different places grow different kinds of goods. Some places have lots of food, others have clothes, and so on. These things help people survive and enjoy life more.

Trade and buying things are blessings when done properly, following God’s rules. People value many items just because they want them, but God lets us enjoy these things too.

But when someone gets rich, they often start to care more about money than God, who gave them the ability to earn it.

27.o: The most mighty and magnificent kingdoms and states, sooner or later, come down. Those who make creatures their confidence, and rest their hopes upon them, will fall with them: happy are those who have the God of Jacob for their Help, and whose hope is in the Lord their God, who lives for ever. Those who engage in trade should learn to conduct their business according to God's word. Those who possess wealth should remember they are the Lord's stewards, and should use his goods in doing good to all. Let us seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness.
27.m:
Powerful kingdoms and great nations will eventually fall. People who trust in things instead of God will fall too. It’s better to trust in the God of Jacob, who always helps his people.

Those who do business should run it by God’s rules. Wealthy people need to remember they are just managers of what belongs to God. They should use their money to help others.

We should put God’s kingdom and goodness first in our lives.

28.o: Ethbaal, or Ithobal, was the prince or king of Tyre; and being lifted up with excessive pride, he claimed Divine honours. Pride is peculiarly the sin of our fallen nature. Nor can any wisdom, except that which the Lord gives, lead to happiness in this world or in that which is to come. The haughty prince of Tyre thought he was able to protect his people by his own power, and considered himself as equal to the inhabitants of heaven. If it were possible to dwell in the garden of Eden, or even to enter heaven, no solid happiness could be enjoyed without a humble, holy, and spiritual mind. Especially all spiritual pride is of the devil. Those who indulge therein must expect to perish.
28.m:
Ethbaal, or Ithobal, was the ruler of Tyre. He became very proud and wanted people to worship him like a god. Pride is a common sin for people after they fell from God’s grace. Only the wisdom God gives can truly bring happiness here on Earth and in the future life.

The proud ruler of Tyre believed he could protect his people using only his own strength. He thought he was just as important as the angels in heaven.
